fields.description: Our hardware designers project water level and quality sensors, dispense actuation systems, and physical interfaces of the tank, alongside communications and radio.
Composed of two teams, this department works on the coding that powers it all, from page you're seeing right now down to the firmware that keeps the sensors talking to one another!
Location selection (figuring out where tanks should go) and how many people each configuration serves. This department calculates useful metrics and figures that help us keep our solution relevant.
Keeping the project sustainable, they track budgets, cost-benefit analysis, material sourcing, and ensure the financial viability of the project.
Managing partnerships with municipalities, regulatory bodies, and institutional partnerships. Responsible for the project's outreach.

fields.title: Progress Update #1
fields.content:
We’ve been working on a new communication architecture for our distributed tank monitoring system, and things are getting exciting! Here’s a quick update on where we are, what we’re testing, and why this approach is going to save us **cost, complexity, and headaches**!!!

---

## 🔗 Moving to BLE for Sensor Communication

We’re exploring **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** as the backbone for communication between sensors and microcontrollers.

Why BLE?

- ✅ Eliminates tons of wiring inside each deposit  
- ✅ Allows sensors to communicate **directly with the microcontroller**  
- ✅ Low power consumption = perfect for long-term deployments  
- ✅ Enables **local communication between tanks**

This last point is huge. Imagine this scenario:

> Tank 1 detects it's empty → it immediately notifies Tank 2 nearby.

That kind of **local awareness** opens the door to smarter system behavior and faster response times without relying on the cloud for everything!

---

## 🧠 Node-Based Architecture: One Brain per Location

Instead of giving **each tank its own board**, we’re designing a **node-based architecture** where:

- Each *location* (site) has **one microcontroller**
- All tanks in that site connect to that microcontroller
- Tanks behave as **slaves**
- The central microcontroller acts as the **master**

### In simple terms:

👉 One **master tank (node controller)** talks to the network  
👉 All other tanks send their data to the master  
👉 The master aggregates and transmits everything externally

This drastically reduces hardware needs!!!

---

## 💸 Cost Savings & Scalability

Let’s say we have **9 tanks in one site** (e.g., Oriente Metro installation):

➡️ Old approach: 9 tanks = 9 boards  
➡️ New approach: 9 tanks = **1 board**

That’s a **massive cost reduction** in hardware, installation, and maintenance!!!

---

## 🧩 Modular External Communications

We’re also designing a **modular external communications unit** that:

- Is **not fixed to any specific tank**
- Exists **once per location**
- Can be replaced independently in case of failure

### Why this matters:

- 🔧 Easier maintenance (swap the module, not the tank system)
- 🔻 Fewer total components
- 🔒 Fewer points of failure
- 🧪 Easier debugging and testing

---

## 📡 Communications Protocol (Yes… RCI is Coming 😅)

Of course, with all this distributed communication, we need to:

- Structure our data into **packets**
- Define **clear communication protocols**
- Handle **synchronization and reliability**

So yes… we’re stepping into **RCI territory** 👀

But this also means we’ll have a **robust, scalable communication layer** from day one!!!

---

## 🛠️ What’s Next?

Here’s what we’re currently working on:

- [ ] BLE module selection and testing  
- [ ] Defining master-slave message structure  
- [ ] Designing packet formats  
- [ ] Prototyping single-node multi-tank setup  
- [ ] Failure-mode testing (what happens when master fails?)  

---

## 🎯 Big Picture

This new architecture will give us:

✨ Lower costs  
✨ Cleaner installations  
✨ Easier maintenance  
✨ More scalable deployments  
✨ Smarter tank-to-tank interaction  

And honestly… it’s just a **much more elegant system overall**!!!

---

We’ll keep sharing updates as we prototype and validate everything in the field. Things are moving fast — stay tuned!! 🚀

fields.title: Site is Live!
fields.content:
Today we’re launching the **first version of our platform** — both a **website and an operational interface** for a modular, resilient water distribution system designed for cities under stress.

This release marks an important step: moving from an abstract concept into something **visible, navigable, and technically grounded**. It’s the first time the project exists as a **real product layer**, built to communicate, manage, and eventually operate an urban emergency water network.

---

## The idea behind the platform 💡

Urban water systems are typically centralized, rigid, and optimized for normal conditions.  
But the world is no longer operating under “normal conditions”.

Conflicts, earthquakes, droughts, and contamination events have shown how quickly access to drinking water can collapse. In those first **72 hours after disruption**, access to safe water becomes not just a logistical issue, but a matter of **public safety, health, and stability**.

Our response is a **redundant, modular, point-to-point water system** that can be deployed across the city and activated whenever the main network fails. The platform you see in Version 1 is the **digital backbone** that makes that system manageable.

---

## More than a website — a control layer 🧠

The site is designed not just as a landing page, but as a **future operational portal**.

Even in this first version, the structure reflects that vision:

It introduces the motivation, the risks, and the solution in a clear and visual way, while also laying the groundwork for a **role-based web interface** where different actors — municipalities, civil protection, emergency services — can access different layers of information.

In future iterations, this same platform will allow operators to **monitor node status, storage levels, quality indicators, and distribution points in real time**.

---

## Built with Django ⚙️

From a technical perspective, Version 1 is powered by **Django**, chosen for its robustness, scalability, and strong ecosystem for building data-driven platforms.

Django gives us:

- a solid MVC structure for clean separation between logic, data, and presentation  
- a powerful admin interface that will later evolve into an internal control dashboard  
- secure authentication and permission management out of the box  
- the flexibility to grow from a static informational site into a full operational platform  

The templating system is already structured to support **component-based sections**, allowing us to iterate quickly on design while maintaining consistency across the site.

---

## Geospatial intelligence with PostGIS 🗺️

Because this system is inherently **spatial**, geography is at the core of everything we’re building.

That’s why we are integrating **PostgreSQL with PostGIS** from the very beginning.

This allows us to:

- store the location of each modular water node  
- map distribution zones and coverage areas  
- analyse proximity to critical infrastructure (hospitals, shelters, dense neighbourhoods)  
- simulate service gaps in the event of network failure  
- plan optimal deployment locations in advance  

In later versions of the platform, this will evolve into an **interactive map interface**, where users can visualize the network, identify active distribution points, and understand coverage in real time.

---

## Designed for growth 🚀

Version 1 is intentionally **lightweight in functionality but strong in structure**.

The design system, layout components, and data architecture are all prepared to scale into:

- real-time monitoring dashboards  
- activation workflows for emergency scenarios  
- integration with IoT sensors and telemetry  
- multi-role access layers for institutions and responders  
- public-facing interfaces for citizens during crises  

Everything you see now is the **foundation layer** for that evolution.

---

## A system for crisis — and for normal times 🔄

Although the platform is built for **catastrophic scenarios**, the system is also designed to be useful in everyday operations.

During normal conditions, these modular water nodes can support **firefighters, civil protection units, and temporary operations in areas where water access is limited**.

This dual use ensures the system is **maintained, tested, and ready**, instead of sitting idle waiting for a disaster.

---

## What comes next 🔭

With this first version live, the next steps are already underway.

We’re working toward integrating **live data simulation**, building the **map-based interface powered by PostGIS**, and expanding the backend into a full **operational dashboard for authorities and emergency services**.

At the same time, we’re continuing to refine the physical deployment model and exploring partnerships for **real-world pilot testing**.

---

## A final thought 💧

Water is the most basic public resource — yet in times of crisis, it becomes one of the most fragile.

We believe cities need **redundant, decentralized, and intelligent systems** to guarantee access when the unexpected happens.

This first version of the platform is a small but concrete step toward that future.

**Thanks for being part of the journey.**
More updates soon.
